meta.title: Fix the broken product page
meta.description: An agent repairs a one-page keyboard store: a product image that won't load and a price that renders as $NaN. Token counts are hand-tuned estimates, not exact.

tools.name: read_file
tools.description: Open a file and see exactly what's inside.

tools.name: list_files
tools.description: See what files exist in a folder.

tools.name: edit_file
tools.description: Replace a piece of text in a file with new text.

tools.name: render_page
tools.description: Rebuild the page and look at the result.

files.page.html: Aurora 65 — Mechanical Keyboard

events.summary: You are a coding agent. You can read files, edit files, and re-render the page.

events.text: My product page is broken — the picture won't load and the price looks wrong. Can you fix it?
Two symptoms: a picture that won't load, and a price that looks wrong. Before changing anything, I should look at the page itself — page.html is where both would show up.

events.output: Aurora 65 — Mechanical Keyboard

events.text: Found a suspect. The image tag points to images/keybord.jpg — "keybord" looks like a typo. But I shouldn't assume. Let me check what the images folder actually contains.
Confirmed. The file on disk is keyboard.jpg, but the page asks for keybord.jpg. The browser was told to load a file that doesn't exist. This is a one-character fix.

files.page.html: Aurora 65 — Mechanical Keyboard

events.text: The image should load now. On to the price: the page shows $NaN, and the number comes from price.js. Time to read that file.

There's the bug. The code adds basePrice and tax — but tax was declared and never given a value. Asking JavaScript to add 129 to "nothing" doesn't fail loudly; it quietly answers NaN — "not a number" — and the page prints exactly that. The fix is to give tax a real value.

files.page.html: Aurora 65 — Mechanical Keyboard

events.output: Page rendered. Image loads: images/keyboard.jpg (OK). Price displays: $149.

events.text: Both problems are fixed. The picture failed because the page asked for keybord.jpg while the file is actually named keyboard.jpg — a one-letter typo. The price showed $NaN because the tax was never given a value, so the math had nothing to add. The page now shows the keyboard and a price of $149.
